name: The Lord's prayer
notes: The pattern of prayer the Lord gave, in two settings: within the Sermon on the Mount in Matthew, and in Luke when a disciple asks "teach us to pray." Matthew gives the fuller form and closes with the doxology "For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory" (6:13), which Luke does not carry. Luke gathers the opening petitions into a single verse (11:2), so Matthew's "Hallowed be thy name" and "Thy kingdom come" both answer to it. The small wording differences — "debts" and "debtors" against "sins" and "every one that is indebted," "this day" against "day by day" — are the two occasions remembered, and are left as matters for study.

links.label: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name
Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done ... in heaven ... earth
forgive us our debts / sins, as we forgive
lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil
